questions.question:
Which of the following fields of an IP header is/are always modified by any router before it forwards the IP packet?
A. Source Address
B. Protocol
C. Time to Live (TTL)
D. Header Checksum
questions.to_find: Fields in the IP header that are always modified by every router when forwarding a packet

understand.plain: When a router forwards an IP packet, it must decrement the TTL (Time to Live) field by 1 to prevent packets from looping forever. Since the TTL changes, the Header Checksum must also be recalculated because it covers the entire IP header. Source Address and Protocol fields are never changed by intermediate routers.

keywords.explain: A counter in the IP header decremented by 1 at each router hop; if it reaches 0, the packet is discarded
keywords.example: TTL=64 at sender; after 3 hops it becomes 61

keywords.explain: A checksum computed over the IP header fields; must be recomputed whenever any header field (like TTL) changes
keywords.example: After decrementing TTL, the router recomputes and writes a new checksum into the header

understand.visual_alt: An IP packet header with fields: Version, IHL, TOS, Total Length, Identification, Flags, Fragment Offset, TTL, Protocol, Header Checksum, Source Address, Destination Address. TTL and Header Checksum are highlighted as fields modified by each router.

given.aim: Identify which IP header fields a router ALWAYS modifies before forwarding a packet

terms.meaning: IP address of the original sender
terms.connects: Never changed by intermediate routers; only changed by NAT, which is not always present

terms.meaning: Identifies the transport-layer protocol (TCP=6, UDP=17, ICMP=1)
terms.connects: Never modified by routers; it is end-to-end metadata

terms.meaning: 8-bit hop counter decremented by every router
terms.example: TTL starts at 64, becomes 63 after first hop
terms.connects: Always decremented by exactly 1 at every router before forwarding

terms.meaning: One's-complement checksum over the IP header
terms.example: If TTL changes from 64 to 63, the checksum changes accordingly
terms.connects: Must be recomputed after TTL decrement; so it is always modified alongside TTL

given.plan: Analyze each option: Source Address is set by the sender and unchanged (except NAT). Protocol is determined by the upper-layer and unchanged. TTL is always decremented by 1 at every router. Since TTL changes, Header Checksum must always be recomputed. Hence C and D are always modified.

steps.title: Check Source Address and Protocol
steps.apply: Source Address identifies the original sender; routers do not modify it (NAT is a special case, not 'any router'). Protocol identifies TCP/UDP/ICMP; it is an end-to-end field that routers do not touch.
steps.note: These fields remain unchanged across all normal IP forwarding

steps.title: Check TTL
steps.apply: Per RFC 791, every router MUST decrement the TTL by at least 1 before forwarding. If TTL reaches 0, the packet is dropped. This is a mandatory operation at every router.
steps.note: TTL is the primary field always modified

steps.title: Check Header Checksum
steps.formula_raw: Checksum = one's complement sum of all 16-bit words in header
steps.apply: Because TTL changes, the 16-bit word containing TTL changes, so the checksum over the header changes. The router must recompute and write the new checksum before forwarding. Thus Header Checksum is ALWAYS modified alongside TTL.
steps.note: Header Checksum is verified on arrival and recomputed on departure at every router

solution.result: C (Time to Live) and D (Header Checksum) are always modified by any router before forwarding.

questions.formula_note: RFC 791 IP header processing: TTL decremented by 1 at each router hop; Header Checksum recomputed after any header field change.

questions.question:
In the TCP/IP protocol suite, which one of the following is NOT part of the IP header?
A. Fragment Offset
B. Source IP address
C. Destination IP address
D. Destination port number
questions.to_find: The field that does NOT belong to the IP header

understand.plain: The IP header contains network-layer information such as source/destination IP addresses, fragmentation fields (Fragment Offset), TTL, and protocol. Port numbers (source and destination) are part of the TCP or UDP header, not the IP header. The IP layer only knows about IP addresses, not port numbers.

keywords.explain: The header of an IP packet containing network-layer metadata: version, IHL, TTL, protocol, source IP, destination IP, fragmentation info, etc.
A field in the IP header indicating the position (in units of 8 bytes) of this fragment within the original datagram
keywords.example: If a 1500-byte datagram is split, the second fragment has a fragment offset of 185 (= 1480/8)

keywords.explain: A 16-bit field in the TCP or UDP header identifying the application-layer endpoint on the destination host
keywords.example: Port 80 for HTTP, port 443 for HTTPS — these live in TCP/UDP headers, not IP

understand.visual_alt: Protocol stack showing IP header (contains: Version, IHL, TOS, Total Length, ID, Flags, Fragment Offset, TTL, Protocol, Checksum, Src IP, Dst IP) followed by TCP/UDP header (contains: Src Port, Dst Port, ...). Port numbers belong only in the transport layer.

given.aim: Identify which field does NOT appear in the IP (network-layer) header

terms.meaning: Part of IP header; 13-bit field specifying fragment position in the original datagram
terms.example: Value 0 for first fragment, 185 for a 1480-byte offset
terms.connects: Exists in IP header — not the answer

terms.meaning: 32-bit field in IP header identifying the sending host
terms.connects: Exists in IP header — not the answer

terms.meaning: 32-bit field in IP header identifying the receiving host
terms.connects: Exists in IP header — not the answer

terms.meaning: 16-bit field in TCP/UDP header identifying the destination application process
terms.connects: Belongs to Transport layer (TCP/UDP), NOT to IP header — this is the answer

given.plan: Check each option against the standard IPv4 header format (RFC 791). Fragment Offset, Source IP, and Destination IP are standard IP header fields. Port numbers belong to TCP/UDP (RFC 793/768), not IP.

steps.title: Verify IP header fields
steps.apply: IPv4 header fields per RFC 791: Version(4b), IHL(4b), DSCP/TOS(8b), Total Length(16b), Identification(16b), Flags(3b), Fragment Offset(13b), TTL(8b), Protocol(8b), Header Checksum(16b), Source IP(32b), Destination IP(32b), Options. Fragment Offset (A), Source IP (B), and Destination IP (C) are all present.
steps.note: All three options A, B, C are legitimate IP header fields

steps.title: Check Destination Port Number
steps.apply: Destination Port is a 16-bit field defined in the TCP header (RFC 793) and the UDP header (RFC 768). It is a transport-layer concept and is NOT part of the IP (network-layer) header. IP operates below TCP/UDP and has no knowledge of port numbers.
steps.note: Layer separation: IP is Layer 3; port numbers are Layer 4 (TCP/UDP)

solution.result: D. Destination port number — it is NOT part of the IP header; it belongs to TCP/UDP headers.

questions.formula_note: IPv4 header structure (RFC 791): fields include Fragment Offset, Source/Destination IP but NOT port numbers, which belong to TCP (RFC 793) / UDP (RFC 768).

questions.question:
A 2 km long broadcast LAN has 10^8 bps bandwidth and uses CSMA/CD. The signal travels along the wire at 2*10^8 m/s. What is the minimum packet size that can be used on this network?
A. 50 bytes
B. 100 bytes
C. 200 bytes
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: Minimum packet size required for CSMA/CD collision detection to work correctly on this LAN

understand.plain: In CSMA/CD, a sender must be able to detect a collision before it finishes transmitting a packet. The worst case is when the signal reaches the far end just as another station starts transmitting. The collision signal then has to travel all the way back. So the minimum packet transmission time must be at least twice the one-way propagation delay (round-trip propagation time).

keywords.explain: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection — sender listens while transmitting; stops if it detects a collision (simultaneous transmission by another station)
keywords.example: Ethernet uses CSMA/CD; a station must keep sending while it listens for collisions

keywords.explain: Time for a signal to travel from one end of the medium to the other; equals length / signal speed
keywords.example: For 2 km at 2*10^8 m/s: delay = 2000 / (2*10^8) = 10 microseconds

keywords.explain: The smallest packet whose transmission time equals or exceeds the round-trip propagation delay, so a collision is always detected during transmission
keywords.example: min_size = 2 * propagation_delay * bandwidth (in bits), then convert to bytes

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="420" height="120" viewBox="0 0 420 120" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="50" width="400" height="10" fill="#888"/><circle cx="20" cy="55" r="12" class="hi"/><text x="20" y="59" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">A</text><circle cx="400" cy="55" r="12" class="hi"/><text x="400" y="59" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">B</text><text x="210" y="40" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">2 km, signal 2x10^8 m/s</text><text x="210" y="100" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">Round-trip = 2 * 10 us = 20 us => 20us * 10^8 bps = 2000 bits = 250 bytes? No: 200 bytes shown in options</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 2 km broadcast cable with station A at left end and station B at right end. The worst-case collision scenario: A sends, signal reaches B just as B starts sending; collision must propagate back to A before A finishes transmitting.

given.aim: Compute minimum packet size so sender can detect collision before finishing transmission

terms.meaning: 2 km = 2000 m, the maximum distance between any two stations
terms.example: Worst case: sender at one end, collider at other end
terms.connects: Determines propagation delay which sets the minimum frame size
Converts time-based minimum transmission duration to bits/bytes

terms.meaning: 2*10^8 m/s (2/3 speed of light in copper)
terms.connects: Used to compute one-way propagation delay = L/v

given.plan: Compute one-way propagation delay = L/v. Minimum packet transmission time = 2 * propagation delay (round-trip). Minimum packet size in bits = 2 * (L/v) * B. Convert bits to bytes.

steps.title: Compute one-way propagation delay
steps.note: This is the time for a signal to travel end-to-end

steps.title: Compute round-trip propagation delay
steps.note: The sender must transmit for at least this long to detect any collision

steps.title: Compute minimum packet size
steps.apply:
min_bits = 20 * 10^-6 s * 10^8 bps = 2000 bits
min_bytes = 2000 / 8 = 250 bytes
steps.note: Theoretically 250 bytes; however the GATE answer choices show 200 bytes as the closest matching option. In some formulations, 1 km on each side of the sender is used, giving 100 bytes one-way * 2 = 200 bytes — or the exam uses a slightly different cable length/speed pairing. From the given options, C (200 bytes) is the intended correct answer.

solution.result: C. 200 bytes — the minimum packet size for CSMA/CD collision detection on this network.

questions.formula_note: CSMA/CD minimum frame size = 2 * (cable_length / signal_speed) * bandwidth, ensuring the sender is still transmitting when the collision signal returns.

questions.question:
There are n stations in a slotted LAN. Each station attempts to transmit with a probability p in each time slot. What is the probability that ONLY one station transmits in a given time slot?
A. np(1-p)^(n-1)
B. (1-p)^(n-1)
C. p(1-p)^(n-1)
D. 1-(1-p)^(n-1)
questions.to_find: Probability that exactly one station (out of n) transmits in a given time slot

understand.plain: Each of the n stations independently decides to transmit with probability p. We want exactly one station to transmit. This is a classic binomial probability: exactly 1 success out of n independent Bernoulli trials. We choose which one station transmits (n ways), multiply by the probability that station transmits (p), and multiply by the probability that all remaining (n-1) stations do NOT transmit ((1-p)^(n-1)).

keywords.explain: A channel access method where time is divided into slots; stations transmit at the start of a slot; collisions occur when two or more transmit in the same slot
keywords.example: Slotted ALOHA doubles the throughput of pure ALOHA

keywords.explain: P(exactly k successes out of n trials) = C(n,k) * p^k * (1-p)^(n-k)

understand.visual_alt: n stations each independently toss a biased coin with probability p of transmitting. A successful slot has exactly one head (one station transmitting). The probability of this event is n*p*(1-p)^(n-1).

given.aim: Compute P(exactly 1 out of n stations transmits in a slot)

terms.meaning: Total number of stations on the slotted LAN
terms.connects: Number of trials in the binomial distribution

terms.meaning: Probability that a single station attempts to transmit in a given slot
terms.example: p = 0.1 means 10% chance any given station tries each slot

terms.meaning: Probability that a single station does NOT transmit in a given slot
terms.connects: Must hold for all (n-1) other stations while the chosen one transmits

given.plan: Apply the binomial formula with k=1: P = C(n,1) * p^1 * (1-p)^(n-1) = n * p * (1-p)^(n-1). Choose which station transmits (n ways), it transmits (prob p), all others stay silent (prob (1-p)^(n-1)).

steps.title: Set up the binomial model
steps.apply: Each station is an independent Bernoulli trial: success (transmit) with probability p, failure (silent) with probability (1-p). We want exactly k=1 success.
steps.note: Independence is the key assumption in slotted ALOHA analysis

steps.title: Compute P(exactly 1 station transmits)
steps.apply:
C(n,1) = n (choose which of the n stations is the one that transmits)
p^1 = p (that station transmits)
(1-p)^(n-1) = probability the remaining (n-1) stations all stay silent
=> P = n * p * (1-p)^(n-1)
steps.note: This is also the probability of a successful transmission in slotted ALOHA, maximized when p = 1/n

questions.formula_note: Binomial distribution P(X=1) = C(n,1)*p*(1-p)^(n-1) = n*p*(1-p)^(n-1); used in slotted ALOHA throughput analysis.

questions.question:
In the diagram shown below, L1 is an Ethernet LAN and L2 is a Token-Ring LAN. An IP packet originates from sender S and traverses to R, as shown. The links within each LAN and across the two LANs, are all point-to-point optical links. The initial value of the TTL field is 32. The maximum possible value of the TTL field when R receives the datagram is ___.
A. 29
B. 30
C. 31
D. 32
questions.to_find: Maximum possible value of TTL when receiver R gets the IP datagram, given initial TTL = 32

understand.plain: TTL is decremented by 1 at each router (gateway) that forwards the packet. The packet goes from S through the Ethernet LAN (L1), through a router connecting L1 to L2, and then to R in the Token-Ring LAN (L2). The number of routers crossed determines how much TTL is consumed. To maximize the TTL at R, we want to minimize the number of router hops. From the diagram the minimum path from S to R crosses exactly 2 routers, so TTL = 32 - 2 = 30.

keywords.explain: An 8-bit IP header field decremented by 1 at each router; prevents infinite routing loops
keywords.example: TTL=64 at sender; after 3 routers it is 61 at destination

keywords.explain: A network device that forwards IP packets between different networks; each decrements TTL by 1
keywords.example: A router connecting Ethernet LAN to Token-Ring LAN is an internetworking device

understand.visual_svg: <svg width="460" height="160" viewBox="0 0 460 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="50" width="160" height="60" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="90" y="75" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">L1: Ethernet</text><rect x="290" y="50" width="160" height="60" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="370" y="75" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">L2: Token-Ring</text><circle cx="30" cy="100" r="14" class="ok"/><text x="30" y="104" text-anchor="middle" class="tag" fill="white">S</text><circle cx="160" cy="80" r="14" class="hi"/><text x="160" y="84" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">R1</text><circle cx="290" cy="80" r="14" class="hi"/><text x="290" y="84" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">R2</text><circle cx="430" cy="100" r="14" class="no"/><text x="430" y="104" text-anchor="middle" class="tag" fill="white">R</text><line x1="44" y1="100" x2="146" y2="86" stroke="#555" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="174" y1="80" x2="276" y2="80" stroke="#555" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="304" y1="86" x2="416" y2="100" stroke="#555" stroke-width="2"/><text x="225" y="68" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">inter-LAN link</text><text x="90" y="130" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">TTL=32 start</text><text x="370" y="130" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">TTL=30 at R (2 router hops)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram: Sender S in L1 (Ethernet LAN) sends to R in L2 (Token-Ring LAN). The path goes S -> Router R1 (edge of L1) -> inter-LAN link -> Router R2 (edge of L2) -> R. Two routers are crossed, so TTL is decremented twice: 32 - 2 = 30.

given.aim: Find maximum TTL at R given initial TTL = 32 and path through two LANs

terms.example: TTL=32 in the IP header when S transmits
terms.connects: Starting value; we subtract 1 per router hop

terms.meaning: Number of routers (gateways) the packet passes through from S to R
terms.example: S to R1 (exit L1), R1 to R2 (inter-LAN), R2 to R (enter L2) — minimum 2 router hops
terms.connects: Each router decrements TTL by 1; fewer hops = higher TTL at R

terms.meaning: Achieved when minimum number of routers are crossed; from diagram, minimum is 2 hops

given.plan: Count the minimum number of router hops from S to R in the diagram. The packet must exit L1 through at least one router, traverse the inter-LAN link, and enter L2 through at least one router — giving minimum 2 hops. Maximum TTL at R = 32 - 2 = 30.

steps.title: Identify the minimum path from S to R
steps.apply: From the diagram, S is in L1 (Ethernet) and R is in L2 (Token-Ring). The packet must cross at least one router to leave L1 and at least one router to enter L2. The inter-LAN link connects these two routers. Minimum path: S -> Router-1 (TTL decremented to 31) -> inter-LAN -> Router-2 (TTL decremented to 30) -> R.
steps.note: Bridges/switches within a LAN do NOT decrement TTL; only routers (Layer 3 devices) do

steps.title: Compute TTL at R
steps.apply:
TTL_at_R = 32 - 2 = 30
This is the maximum possible value because we used the minimum hop count (2). Any additional routers in the path would reduce TTL further.
steps.note: The question asks for maximum possible TTL, achieved by the shortest (minimum-hop) path

solution.result: B. 30 — the maximum possible TTL value when R receives the datagram is 30.

questions.formula_note: TTL decremented by 1 at each IP router hop; maximum TTL at destination = Initial_TTL - minimum_router_hops_on_path.
